--- SOURCE 1: Assessing the barriers and implications of autonomous vehicles ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340

SUMMARY:
The emergence of autonomous vehicles (AVs) promises to revolutionize urban transportation within sustainable cities by leveraging advanced technologies to improve traffic flow, reduce emissions, enhance safety, and increase mobility. To unify the objectives and provide a clear focus, the study's primary research question is, what are the key barriers to AV adoption, and how can their integration into urban transportation systems be aligned with societal, economic, and environmental sustainability goals? Assessing the barriers and implications of autonomous vehicles: Implementation in sustainable cities
====================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Irfan Ullah a b, Jianfeng Zheng c, Alessandro Severino d, Arshad Jamal e

Show more

Abstract
--------

The emergence of autonomous vehicles (AVs) promises to revolutionize urban transportation within sustainable cities by leveraging advanced technologies to improve traffic flow, reduce emissions, enhance safety, and increase mobility. However, significant barriers exist, such as ensuring AV safety and reliability, requiring extensive infrastructure investments, and addressing complex implications like employment shifts, travel behavior changes, and ethical concerns. This paper explores recent AV developments, highlights key challenges, and discusses their broader implications. Examining these factors aims to enhance understanding of AV technology and its future impact on sustainable urban transportation.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001376)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001029)

Keywords
--------

Autonomous vehicles

Sustainable cities

Future mobility

Traffic safety

Traffic management

Intelligent transport system

1. Introduction
---------------

The concept of Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) dates back to the early 20th century, with initial experiments in radio-controlled cars in the 1920s. However, significant advancements began in the 1980s with the development of AI-driven navigation systems [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0001)]. Landmark moments include Carnegie Mellon University's Navlab project, DARPA's Grand Challenge in the 2000s, and Google's launch of its self-driving car initiative in 2010, now known as Waymo [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0002)]. Over the years, major automakers and tech companies have invested heavily in AV technology, driving rapid innovation. While AVs hold the promise of transforming transportation by enhancing safety, reducing congestion, and lowering emissions, challenges related to regulation, safety, and public acceptance remain key hurdles to widespread adoption [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0003)].

The concept of AVs dates back to the early 20th century, with significant advancements occurring over the decades. As AV technology progresses, its integration into sustainable cities brings about unique barriers and implications, shaped by the different stages of technological development. These stages not only define the capabilities of AVs but also influence the challenges related to infrastructure, regulation, societal acceptance, and technological readiness. The Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) defines six levels of AV automation, ranging from Level 0 (No Automation) where the driver retains full control, to Level 5 (Full Automation) where vehicles operate entirely without human intervention ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#fig0001)) [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0004)]. As AVs advance through these levels, the complexity of integrating them into urban environments increases, necessitating changes in urban planning, transportation policies, and societal adaptation. These levels of automation significantly impact the scope of changes required for successful AV implementation in cities, influencing everything from infrastructure to social readiness.

![Image 10: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666188825001340-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (413KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666188825001340-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (413KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666188825001340-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Driving automation level as proposed by the SAE.

Recent advancements in information and communication technology (ICT) offer innovative opportunities to enhance the efficiency and management of existing transportation networks. However, their full potential often requires complementary infrastructure and capacity improvements. From the operational to strategic level, these advances augment the efficacy and reliability of transportation systems and facilitate the driver's decision-making process [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0005)]. AVs are an emerging vehicle technology expected to revolutionize transportation in the 21st century [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0006)]. During the last two decades, AV has been the subject of widespread attention in research studies, government reports, media articles, blogs, and even movies. AVs, also known as self-driving cars or driverless cars, are a rapidly evolving technology that has the potential to revolutionize transportation. The concept of AVs has been around for several decades, but it wasn't until recent technological advancements that they became a reality [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0007)]. These vehicles use different sensors, radar, cameras, and lidar to perceive their surrounds and make decisions based on that information. Machine learning and artificial intelligence are also critical components of AV technology, enabling vehicles to adapt and learn from their experiences. However, comprehensive studies on the broader impacts of AVs are limited. AVs are broadly categorized into cooperative and autonomous types. Cooperative AVs can communicate with other vehicles and infrastructure unlike their fully autonomous counterparts. This capability enhances their ability to sense and anticipate the actions of vehicles ahead, thereby influencing traffic flow [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0008)]. As AV technology is still developing, mixed traffic conditions, with both traditional and cooperative AVs, will likely persist for some time. During this initial phase, various levels of automation, from partial to full driverless systems, will coexist [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0004)].

Consequently, much research is devoted to understanding the potential effects of AVs on traffic dynamics. The potential benefits of AVs are significant. One of the most essential benefits is improved safety [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0009)]. AVs are less prone to human error, which is responsible for the vast majority of accidents on the road. They can also communicate with each other and the infrastructure around them, which could help reduce accidents caused by unexpected events or driver mistakes. AVs can potentially enhance road efficiency and reduce congestion [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0007)] significantly. They can travel closer together in a platoon-like formation and can use real-time data to adjust their speed and routes to avoid traffic jams. Additionally, AVs offer enhanced transportation access for individuals unable to drive, including the elderly and those with disabilities [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0010)].

However, the advancement and implementation of AVs also pose significant technical, legal, and regulatory challenges [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0011)]. One of the biggest technical challenges is the need for highly accurate and reliable sensing and mapping technologies [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0012)]. AVs must be able to perceive and understand their surroundings in real-time, in various weather and lighting conditions. They must also navigate safely and efficiently without getting lost or confused by complex road infrastructure. Achieving this level of capability requires significant advancements in hardware and software technologies [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0013)]. Another challenge is the need to establish a legal and regulatory framework that ensures the safety and accountability of AVs [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0005)]. Governments must create regulations that ensure that AVs are safe, secure, and reliable. They must also establish liability frameworks that determine who is responsible in the event of an accident involving an AVs. Finally, they must establish standards for the testing and deploying AVs, to ensure that they meet the necessary safety and performance requirements [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0014)].

Despite these challenges, the development of AV technology is advancing rapidly. Numerous companies, including major automakers and technology giants, are investing heavily in the development of AV technology. They are conducting extensive testing and research to improve the technology and address the challenges that come with it. Governments are also taking steps to support the development of AVs, through funding, research partnerships, and regulatory reform [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0015)]. The future of transportation is likely to be shaped by the development and deployment of AVs. As technology advances, a wide range of new transportation options, from autonomous buses and taxis to delivery trucks and drones. AVs can transform our lives and work, providing safer, more efficient, and more accessible transportation options [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0016)]. However, several challenges should be considered before this potential can be fully realized. As a result, researchers, engineers, policymakers, and stakeholders worldwide are working together to advance the development of AV technology and ensure its safe and effective integration into our transportation systems.

The rapid advancement of AV technology has ignited a profound debate surrounding its widespread implementation. This study delves into the intricacies of AV integration, precisely evaluating the obstacles and potential consequences accompanying this transformative leap in transportation. The primary objective is to identify the multifaceted challenges and barriers that may impede the seamless integration of AVs into the existing transportation infrastructure. This comprehensive assessment will encompass technological hurdles, regulatory frameworks, societal acceptance, and ethical considerations with self-driving vehicles.

Furthermore, the study will meticulously assess the potential consequences of AV deployment on diverse facets of society, including the economy, the environment, and the social landscape. The economic impact of job displacement, new industries, and potential shifts in consumer behavior will be examined. Environmental implications will be evaluated through emissions reduction, traffic congestion alleviation, and possible resource consumption. Societal impacts will be assessed regarding accessibility for individuals with disabilities, potential safety concerns, and the broader implications for human interactions and urban planning. By meticulously examining these factors, this study proffers a holistic understanding of the opportunities and challenges related to AV deployment, thereby apprising policymakers and stakeholders with the critical insights necessary to navigate this transformative era in transportation. The findings will serve as a valuable resource for informed decision-making, ensuring that the widespread adoption of AVs aligns with societal, economic, and environmental sustainability goals. To unify the objectives and provide a clear focus, the study's primary research question is, what are the key barriers to AV adoption, and how can their integration into urban transportation systems be aligned with societal, economic, and environmental sustainability goals?

So, this study aims to comprehensively analyze these challenges and their broader societal, economic, and environmental impacts, offering insights to guide sustainable integration.
*   1.This study systematically categorizes key obstacles to AV adoption, including technological, regulatory, and societal challenges. 
*   2.Evaluate AV deployment's economic, environmental, and social implications, such as emissions reduction and accessibility improvements. 
*   3.Provides actionable recommendations for integrating AVs into urban transportation systems while aligning with sustainability goals. 
*   4.Establish a structured foundation to guide future studies and industry collaborations in AV development. 

In the subsequent sections of this paper, the focus shifts towards a comprehensive exploration of AVs. [Section 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#sec0002) delves into an in-depth analysis of existing literature on AVs, conducted bibliometric analysis. [Section 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#sec0003) discusses the barriers to AV implementation. [Section 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#sec0007) explores the potential implications of AVs. Following this, [Section 5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#sec0017) examines their social and economic implications. [Section 6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#sec0022) focuses on environmental implications. In [Section 7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#sec0024), addresses policy and planning implications. Finally, in [Section 8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#sec0027), the paper concludes by summarizing key findings and highlighting the broader implications of the research, providing valuable insights into the future of autonomous transportation.

2. Bibliometric analysis
------------------------

Bibliometric analysis plays a crucial role in advancing research by offering a systematic approach that minimizes errors and biases when selecting studies on a specific topic. Additionally, it provides a comprehensive overview of independent research efforts, helping to identify knowledge gaps and limitations [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0017)]. The initial step in bibliometric research involves choosing a reliable and well-established database for analysis. Recently, discussions have emerged regarding the comparability and reliability of statistical data obtained from the two leading databases Web of Science (WoS) and Scopus [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0018)]. Although there is significant overlap between them, Scopus is recognized as the largest citation database covering peer-reviewed literature across multiple disciplines [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0019)]. These databases share many similarities [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0020),[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0021)]. According to Elsevier, Scopus serves as the most extensive citation and abstract database, widely utilized by researchers for bibliometric studies across various fields. With approximately 69 million records, Scopus indexes a greater number of journals compared to WoS [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0022),[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#bib0023)].

Given its broader coverage, this study utilizes the Scopus database to ensure the inclusion of a wide range of relevant articles, including those that may not be indexed in WoS or considered in previous research.

In the search criteria, while "AVs" was a primary search term, we also included variations such as "autonomous vehicles," "self-driving cars," and "driverless cars" to ensure comprehensive coverage. The search was refined to include, only research articles, review articles, and conference papers were included in the analysis and the result were limited to publications from 2000 to 2023, as illustrated in [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#fig0002). In total, 10,119 publications were identified. The inclusion criteria focused on peer-reviewed journal publications to ensure the reliability and academic rigor of the selected studies. The exclusion criteria encompassed document types such as book chapters, notes, errata, editorials, short surveys, conference reviews, books, letters, retracted papers, data papers, and reports, as these sources may not always undergo the same level of peer review or may not align with the study's objectives. Additionally, any duplicate articles identified during the selection process were removed to maintain the integrity and accuracy of the dataset.

![Image 11: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666188825001340-gr2.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (166KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666188825001340-gr2_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (166KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666188825001340-gr2.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 2. Annually published articles on AVs research.

The bibliometric analysis also incorporated filters to focus on the most relevant data points, such as active countries contributing to AV research and the most influential keywords related to AVs. Further, the VOSviewer software was used to examine the sorted data. [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#fig0003) shows the top 10 countries contributing to the AVs research. United States, with over 2422 publications, the United States is by far the most active country in AV research. The country is home to several leading companies and research institutions, including Waymo, Uber, and Carnegie Mellon University. China is the second most active country in AV research, with over 2189 publications. The country has significantly invested in AV technology, with companies like Baidu and Tencent developing their own AVs. The United Kingdom is the third most active country in AV research, with over 511 publications. The country has several leading research institutions, including the University of Oxford and the University of Cambridge. Germany is the fourth most active country in AVs research. The country is home to several leading automotive manufacturers, including BMW, Mercedes-Benz, and Volkswagen, which have all invested heavily in AV technology. Other countries active in AVs research include Canada, France, Spain, and India. Additionally, the data is subject to change over time as new research is published and countries continue to invest in AV technology.

![Image 12: Fig. 3](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666188825001340-gr3.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (250KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666188825001340-gr3_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (250KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666188825001340-gr3.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 3. Analysis of countries productivity.

Keywords are essential elements in research, serving as indicators of the central topics within a study's domain. They provide a quick overview of the subject matter and facilitate literature search and indexing processes. In the context of research on AVs, keywords play a crucial role in delineating the diverse aspects and themes encompassed within this burgeoning field. [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#fig0003) of this study visualizes the most frequently used keywords in research publications related to AVs. By analyzing a comprehensive dataset of scholarly articles, the top 50 keywords have been identified and depicted in the figure. These keywords encapsulate various dimensions of AV research, ranging from technological advancements to societal implications.

Among the prominent keywords highlighted in Figure are 'autonomous vehicles', 'automation', 'intelligent vehicles highway system', 'motion planning', 'decision making', and 'autonomous driving'. Each of these terms represents a fundamental aspect of AV research, reflecting the multifaceted nature of the field. 'Autonomous vehicles' and 'automation', for instance, underscore the core concept of vehicles capable of operating without human intervention, while 'motion planning' and 'decision making' delve into the intricate algorithms and processes involved in guiding AVs through dynamic environments.

Figure shows these keywords' network representation, showcasing their co-occurrence patterns across different research articles. The network visualization reveals four distinct clusters of keywords, each denoted by a different color (red, blue, green, and yellow). These clusters signify that keywords within the same group commonly appear in the literature, suggesting underlying themes or research subdomains. The red cluster featuring terms such as intelligent vehicles highway system, road vehicles, intelligent system, and traffic congestion focuses on the infrastructure and systemic aspects of vehicular networks and road management. The green cluster comprising keywords like computer simulation, navigation, and robotics emphasizes the importance of simulation technologies and robotic systems in enhancing navigational capabilities and testing. In the blue cluster, core autonomous technologies are evident through terms such as autonomous driving, machine learning, deep learning, and artificial intelligence, underscoring the computational methods driving self-driving systems. Finally, the yellow cluster with keywords like controller, steering, and model predictive control highlights the technical elements essential for precise vehicle operation and real-time control. Together, the size of each node reflects its prominence in the literature, while the connecting lines illustrate how frequently these terms co-occur, providing a comprehensive view of how different facets of autonomous vehicle research interconnect to drive innovation.

Overall, [Fig. 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666188825001340#fig0004) is a valuable tool for researchers and practitioners seeking to navigate the vast and evolving field of AV research. It offers a glimpse into diverse topics and perspectives shaping discussions on AVs, highlighting the interconnected nature of research endeavors in this dynamic domain.

![Image 13: Fig. 4](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666188825001340-gr4.jpg)

In the Bibliometric analysis the findings were synthesized by identifying the most active countries in the field of AVs and analyzing the most relevant keywords. This involved mapping research output by country to determine leading contributors and examining keyword co-occurrence to identify dominant themes and emerging trends in AV research. ## Breadcrumb

# Mobility: The Socioeconomic Implications of Autonomous Vehicles

Autonomous vehicle (AV) technology is developing rapidly and is poised to change all aspects of transportation and mobility. AVs are already on the road, some without backup human drivers. Widespread adoption of AVs will change the demand for labor, conceptions of private vehicle ownership, and urban land use. On the one hand, the implementation of AVs promises increased safety, efficiency, and access to transportation. On the other hand, scientists and social scientists predict that AVs will make pollution worse and exacerbate socioeconomic inequality. Policymakers must be prepared for the legislative and regulatory challenges AVs will soon bring.

This memo highlights some of the positive and negative economic, infrastructural, environmental, and socioeconomic implications of the widespread adoption of vehicles with a high level of autonomy. Each section then presents policy suggestions to address the ongoing and coming societal changes. Finally, the memo concludes with a discussion of the technological, regulatory, and ethical barriers to even getting these highly autonomous vehicles on the road as regular consumer products.

--- SOURCE 3: What might be the economic implications of autonomous vehicles? ---
URL: https://economicsobservatory.com/what-might-be-the-economic-implications-of-autonomous-vehicles

SUMMARY:
Autonomous vehicles could change our urban spaces and transport systems by altering commuting patterns, improving safety and decreasing congestion and

FULL CONTENT:
# What might be the economic implications of autonomous vehicles?

### Autonomous vehicles could change our urban spaces and transport systems by altering commuting patterns, improving safety and decreasing congestion and emissions. Rapid advancements in artificial intelligence have the potential to accelerate these trends.

The [Automated Vehicles Act](https://www.legislation.gov.uk/ukpga/2024/10/contents/enacted), which became law on 20 May 2024, means that self-driving vehicles could be on the UK’s roads by 2026. Their introduction – and expansion – could change how people and goods in the UK get around.

Understanding how this new technology interacts with society will be fundamental to assessing its economic impact. Further, the UK government is [seeking to understand](https://nic.org.uk/app/uploads/NIC-CAM-Study-Call-for-Evidence-Final-24-April.pdf) the policies required to ensure that connected autonomous vehicles (AVs) can support sustainable economic growth across all regions of the country and support the transition to net-zero carbon emissions.

AVs are those that can operate and navigate without human intervention, such as self-driving cars, lorries, buses, autonomous delivery robots and drones. The level of automation [ranges from zero (no automation) to five (full automation)](https://www.sae.org/standards/content/j3016_202104/). In contrast with traditional cars, AVs rely on sensors, cameras, radar systems and artificial intelligence to drive, make decisions and create a safer environment that improves mobility.

One [study for the United States](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.3141/2606-14) estimates that the economic gains from AVs could reach $936 billion per year due to savings from reduced accidents and increased productivity.

A similar [study for the UK](https://assets.kpmg.com/content/dam/kpmg/images/2015/05/connected-and-autonomous-vehicles.pdf) estimates that the benefits could reach £51 billion by 2030. These come through consumer savings, such as reduced travel time, lower transport costs, increased telecommunication data traffic, taxation and improved safety.

### How might AVs change transport and mobility?

The transport and logistics sectors are likely to be significantly affected by the introduction and growth of AVs.

AVs are expected to change urban mobility – how people get around in cities – with transport and logistics firms likely to be the first adopters due to opportunities to reduce costs and increase efficiency and safety ([Fagnant and Kockelman, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0965856415000804?via%3Dihub); [Correia and van Arem, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0191261515300692?via%3Dihub)).

One key source of savings comes from the elimination of the need for human drivers, which will significantly reduce labour costs. In addition, firms can save on operational costs, as AVs can reduce maintenance costs and lower insurance premiums and optimise fuel consumption ([Webb, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0313592618300638?casa_token=q6XuyCu4tDQAAAAA:qIPXe-W8JOEigDLi3Gh2sXI1Lwj1-kj2Oi2esNTlA7lUO6FLa2UVIX9kr6MFpxECXILxjZTqIGI)). Fuel consumption savings apply to AVs that use traditional combustion engines – although there is an overlap between AVs and electric vehicles (EVs), some still using petrol/diesel, some hybrid and some powered by hydrogen fuel cells.

AVs can also operate 24/7, resulting in faster deliveries, thus increasing overall productivity and customer satisfaction. The ability to handle higher volumes of goods at all times could reshape the entire logistic landscape, and help firms to cope with higher demands from e-commerce markets ([Rai et al, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2210539521001577?casa_token=7oT6E0EdacAAAAAA:7ArjSSabMoOfdKJg71ST1jYQvDIlV-TWS50P_u3mhpatpxL7jnzLD0REXoIf8G20k-g9eQJZz_w)).

But delivery is not as a simple process as one might think. Last-mile deliveries are known to be one of the greatest challenges for companies and [AVs may offer a potential solution](https://www3.weforum.org/docs/WEF_Future_of_the_last_mile_ecosystem.pdf). This step – when a parcel is moved from the warehouse to its final destination, usually a residence or retail store – is the most expensive part of the delivery process. To keep up with increasing consumer demand for fast delivery, firms need to make it more efficient.

With their ability to optimise delivery routes and operate faster and more efficiently, firms can explore the integration of AVs and drones into their operations to address last-mile delivery challenges and offer improved customer experience. For example, autonomous drones and robots have the capability to deliver packages to remote or inaccessible locations that lack proper transport infrastructure ([Patella et al, 2020](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/13/1/6)).

AVs are also likely to have profound implications for personal mobility. They offer the potential to improve mobility for individuals who are underserved by the current transport system or facing mobility barriers, such as older people, those with disabilities and non-drivers ([Sanbonmatsu et al, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1369847817307118?via%3Dihub)).

AVs also can reduce transport costs, thereby improving mobility for middle and lower-income groups. This enhanced mobility and increased accessibility should allow socially marginalised people to participate more in society in several ways, including accessing employment opportunities and participating in leisure activities ([Faber and Lierop, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0965856419312091)).

Fleet-based shared automated vehicles – provided by private companies – are expected to offer low-cost, on-demand door-to-door services that could complement traditional public transport. Specifically, they could improve access to first- and last-mile connections or substitute for public transport in areas where provision is limited ([Yap et al, 2016](https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S0965856416307765)).

Further, autonomous public transport systems can provide more equitable access for all community members. Automated public transport, such as metros and bus rapid transport (BRT), can improve punctuality and increase the frequency of the service, using the infrastructure more efficiently ([Begg, 2014](https://trid.trb.org/View/1319762)).

What’s more, automated on-demand public transport provides ride-sharing services without a fixed timetable or routes, and that can serve users, particularly as a first- and last-mile connection to the main transport services ([Ainsalu et al, 2018](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/10/9/3118)). This inclusivity can foster better economic opportunities and improved wellbeing.

Despite the potential benefits, AVs are expected to [disrupt employment](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0264275121001013) and sectors such as public transport, taxi and delivery services, potentially resulting in job losses for workers in these industries. As automated systems replace human drivers, there will be a need for retraining programmes to help displaced workers make the transition to new employment opportunities.

This shift may exacerbate existing inequalities, as workers with lower education levels and fewer transferable skills may find it more challenging to adapt to new roles in the labour market. To deal with this disruption, policy-makers should focus on education, training and re-skilling opportunities.

### How can AVs drive economic growth and boost productivity?

At the regional level, AVs could improve accessibility by reducing transport costs. It is anticipated that the widespread adoption of AVs will alleviate congestion and significantly decrease travel times.

This is due to the ability of AVs to communicate and optimise traffic flow, reduce human errors and maintain constant speeds. AVs can use real-time data for optimal route planning, drive closely together safely (what is known as platooning) and integrate with smart traffic management systems. This enhanced efficiency, and reduced incidents, would lead to faster travel experiences.

As transport becomes more efficient and its opportunity cost decreases, individuals may choose to live further from urban centres, potentially increasing the value of properties located away from the city centre and contributing to urban sprawl ([Glaeser and Kahn, 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574008004800130?casa_token=hQMoltK3pO4AAAAA:mJRP-z4giLCS5TxVgw6Y4fzas4J67wtVYriChx3aUHPhjQvC4cxjz7yMveawkvSGyrcvYDFEjXk)). Early research suggests that congestion improvements are more pronounced on motorways, where AVs could increase the existing capacity ([van den Berg and Verhoef, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0191261515300643?casa_token=VcgmYNkDpuEAAAAA:ifJc4x1baM1G9SaFkr9HO1YieIOM9SKaeQ7Fs5QElx-bb2n8JBTElXtC1RW5rZQVs5Gf0lUkTC8)).

These developments in transport have an impact on economic activity and the spatial distribution of firms and individuals, as better connections between an economic centre and its periphery can lead to job and population relocations ([Teulings et al, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0094119017300839?casa_token=1yojgzR3ze0AAAAA:w0r2bcPeUldFk9WdmiutyVypClmifkyGnswqTRvQf6iYQ6hHgiEKIpF6SEbnJ1CgpHX0TyceXrU)).

In this way, a more cost-effective and efficient transport system allows firms and individuals to interact across locations and benefit from the spatial concentration of agglomeration economies, such as increased access to labour markets, knowledge exchange and scale economies ([Laird and Venables, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0967070X16304863?casa_token=AgxelmWy7bUAAAAA:9Qv5hgxcYpH2LoOKirh44qLMfNJMswCKuS0Keu1N5OV5vbvXGrEnyt0MpE3dUNzH2CERHKz8cP4); [Graham and Gibbons, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2212012218300856)). Improved transport enhances firms' productivity by reducing travel costs, providing better access to infrastructure and facilitating gains in production efficiency.

At a broader economic level, one study investigating the impact of AVs on accessibility finds that in an optimistic scenario, AVs are equivalent to 15 years of infrastructure investments, and that well-connected rural areas are more likely to experience the largest connectivity gains, while the effect in city centres is less strong ([Meyer et al, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0739885917300021)). It has become evident that this trend leads to increased urban sprawl ([Glaeser and Khan, 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1574008004800130?casa_token=KvCqGMYuUW0AAAAA:OfvA_yGgFUmDHLfHNWJ1Or0yHp5e8Z-hyUj7Z9YS9is7nrwxkFylv7w4IpesRph_GJnCmT-QK-A)).

Further, research focused on the United States shows that AVs could increase GDP by $214 billion, create 2.4 million new jobs and boost labour income by $90 billion ([Winston and Karpilow, 2020](https://books.google.co.uk/books?hl=en&lr=&id=p4_UDwAAQBAJ&oi=fnd&pg=PP1&dq=Winston,+C.+and+Karpilow,+Q.+(2020).+Autonomous+Vehicles:+The+Road+to+Economic+Growth%3F+Brookings+Institution+Press.&ots=5ATSe7jG06&sig=DAs5yaeB2w7wGSDlumwmzA696rw#v=onepage&q&f=false)).

### What else do we need to know?

Additional potential contributions of AVs are fuel optimisation and carbon emission reduction. By leveraging algorithms to determine the most efficient routes and speeds, AVs could minimise fuel consumption and promote a more sustainable transport system. Integrating electric and hybrid vehicles into the autonomous fleet can further reduce greenhouse gas emissions and air pollution ([Kova?i? et al, 2022](https://www.mdpi.com/2071-1050/14/15/9525)).

As electric vehicle technologies continue to expand, the adoption of AVs could be crucial in mitigating climate change and reducing the environmental impact of transport, helping to meet climate targets.

Nevertheless, the mainstream adoption of AVs also presents some challenges that must be addressed. Establishing public trust and acceptance is a critical factor in the adoption of AVs, as concerns about safety and reliability can hinder progress. To deploy this technology successfully, companies and governments must invest in transparent communication and public education about the benefits of AVs.

In addition, AVs are susceptible to [software glitches](https://www.ft.com/content/f64383d2-89c7-459a-afce-5c659473050f), sensor inaccuracies and cybersecurity threats, such as hacking and malicious activities. [AVs will record everything that happens in and around them](https://www.economist.com/leaders/2018/03/01/self-driving-cars-offer-huge-benefits-but-have-a-dark-side?utm_medium=affiliates.content.pd&utm_source=Skimbit%20Ltd.&utm_campaign=16002&utm_content=Online%20Tracking%20Link&irclickid=QczQHOz0BxyKR7zUPb3vE3Q-UkC2kYy1hRr0WA0&channel=Impact&irgwc=1), raising privacy concerns. Again, collaborative efforts between firms and governments will be necessary to establish robust security measures and protect the integrity of AVs, including encryption and sharing best practices ([Seetharaman et al, 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/pdf/10.1080/08839514.2020.1799149)).

Governments are actively working on developing rigorous regulations and comprehensive legislation for AVs to ensure safety guidelines for manufacturers and consumers. In the UK, the government has launched a plan that could put the country at the [forefront of self-driving technology regulation](https://www.gov.uk/government/news/self-driving-vehicles-set-to-be-on-roads-by-2026-as-automated-vehicles-act-becomes-law#:~:text=Self%2Ddriving%20vehicles%20could%20be%20on%20British%20roads%20by%202026,drive%20vehicles%20on%20British%20roads.).

The AV Act lays the foundation for the future deployment of AVs on public roads by introducing clear objectives to improve road safety and clarify the legal liability associated with this new technology.

Further, AV adoption requires modifications to current infrastructure and urban planning, particularly to digital infrastructure, including designated lanes, intelligent traffic systems and charging structures. Earlier in 2024, [the UK government commissioned a new study on connected AVs](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/chancellor-commissions-new-study-on-connected-and-autonomous-vehicles-and-mobility/chancellor-commissions-new-study-on-connected-and-automated-vehicles-terms-of-reference) to provide recommendations about the infrastructure and policies required to support economic growth across UK regions.

Addressing these challenges is crucial for realising the potential benefits of AVs, including safer roads, reduced congestion, improved accessibility, environmental sustainability and economic opportunities. These advances present a good opportunity to reshape intra- and inter-urban mobility towards a smarter and more connected future.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Developing autonomous vehicles involves substantial investment in advanced technologies, including AI, sensors, and software, which can strain resources and delay market entry.
Investors face significant uncertainties regarding regulatory frameworks, consumer acceptance, and market viability, leading to hesitancy in funding AV initiatives despite their potential benefits.
The race for AV dominance is intensifying, with numerous companies vying for market share, resulting in fierce competition that can impact pricing strategies and innovation rates.
